description:
You are acting as a DevSecOps engineer who must codify a very small “network-exposure” policy.  
A helper has already dropped a raw port inventory file at  

    /home/user/devsecops/policy/inventory.txt  

1) File format that already exists  
   • Each line: <port_number>:<service_name>/<protocol>  
   • Example (spacing shown with ░ only for clarity – there are NO spaces in the file):  
        22:ssh/tcp  
        443:https/tcp  
   • The file actually contains **exactly five (5) lines** following that pattern.

2) Your tasks  
   a. Transform the raw data into a JSON document **/home/user/devsecops/policy/inventory.json**  
      • The JSON root must be an array.  
      • Each element must be an object with keys in this exact order: `"port"`, `"service"`, `"proto"`.  
      • The values must be:  
        - port …… integer (not a string)  
        - service … lowercase string, exactly as it appeared before the slash  
        - proto …… lowercase string, exactly as it appeared after the slash  
      • The array must be **sorted by the numeric value of port in ascending order**.  
      • The file must be pretty-printed with two-space indentation and a trailing newline.

      Example of the precise layout that automated tests will check (values will differ):  
      [  
        {  
          "port": 1,  
          "service": "foo",  
          "proto": "tcp"  
        },  
        {  
          "port": 2,  
          "service": "bar",  
          "proto": "udp"  
        }  
      ]⏎      ← (final newline required)

   b. Produce a minimalist log **/home/user/devsecops/policy/open_ports.log**  
      • Contain only the port numbers, sorted numerically ascending, one per line, no extra characters.  
      • Also end with a single trailing newline.

3) Verification hints (for yourself)  
   • `jq -S` can help ensure key ordering; `sort -n` ensures numeric order.  
   • `cat -e` shows trailing newline as `$`.

You should be able to accomplish everything with no more than 3–4 shell commands (e.g., `awk`, `sort`, `jq`, redirections).  
Make sure both output files exist at the exact paths and with the exact contents/format described; automated tests will read them verbatim.
